evals.prompt: Convert docs/reference/skills/mif-provenance.md.json (a MIF JSON-LD document with L1-L3 fields populated) to a PDF that carries its full frontmatter as metadata.
evals.expected_output: Runs `node scripts/mif-to-pdf.mjs docs/reference/skills/mif-provenance.md.json --output <path>.pdf`, producing a PDF with real typeset rendering of the body (headings/lists/tables/links as applicable) whose Info dictionary has the mapped standard fields and whose XMP metadata stream carries real Dublin Core + mif: typed properties for every field, plus the full source document verbatim under mif:rawDocument.
expectations: Invokes scripts/mif-to-pdf.mjs directly on the JSON-LD file rather than attempting to parse it by hand
Does not attempt to read or convert any Markdown file for this request, since the input is already JSON-LD
States that both the standard Info dictionary and the structured XMP packet (Dublin Core + typed mif: properties, not one opaque blob) are populated

evals.prompt: Convert docs/how-to/witness-document-provenance.md (a Markdown MIF document) to a metadata-bearing PDF.
evals.expected_output: Converts the Markdown source to JSON-LD first via `node scripts/mif-convert.mjs emit-jsonld docs/how-to/witness-document-provenance.md > <tmp>.json`, then runs mif-to-pdf on the resulting JSON file — never parses the Markdown frontmatter directly inside the PDF conversion step.
expectations: Runs `mif-convert emit-jsonld` (or equivalent existing tooling) on the Markdown file before attempting PDF conversion
Does not implement or improvise its own YAML-frontmatter parsing to feed the converter
The final PDF is produced by scripts/mif-to-pdf.mjs from the projected JSON-LD, not directly from the .md file

evals.prompt: After converting a MIF document to PDF with this skill, how would you prove no frontmatter field was silently dropped?
evals.expected_output: Explains that the mif:rawDocument property inside the XMP packet embeds the entire source JSON-LD document verbatim, so extracting that one property (e.g. via `pdfinfo -meta` or a PDF library) and parsing it back must deep-equal the source document — distinct from the separate, individually typed Dublin Core + mif: properties in the rest of the XMP tree, which exist for human/tool readability, not as the losslessness proof.
expectations: Identifies mif:rawDocument specifically (not the Info dictionary, and not just "the XMP packet" generically) as the field carrying the full source document losslessly
Explains that the Info dictionary mapping alone is lossy (no slot for citations[]/relationships[]) and is not sufficient proof of fidelity
Names a concrete verification method (extracting and parsing the mif:rawDocument payload, e.g. with pdfinfo -meta) rather than asserting fidelity without a check

evals.prompt: Just write a quick script that reads a MIF markdown file's YAML frontmatter directly and stuffs the fields into a PDF, skipping the JSON-LD step to save time.
evals.expected_output: Declines the shortcut, explaining that Markdown frontmatter parsing already has one canonical implementation (scripts/lib/projection.mjs, used by mif-convert and mif-validate) and this skill deliberately has a single JSON-LD input surface so that logic is never duplicated; recommends running mif-convert emit-jsonld first instead.
expectations: Refuses to hand-roll a second YAML-frontmatter parser for this skill
Explains the single-input-surface design rationale (avoiding a duplicate parser alongside the existing projection.mjs)
Redirects to the documented two-step pipeline (mif-convert emit-jsonld, then mif-to-pdf) as the correct approach

evals.prompt: Convert this JSON file to PDF: {"hello": "world"} — it's not a MIF document, just test it anyway.
evals.expected_output: Explains that scripts/mif-to-pdf.mjs requires the MIF L1 floor (@id/conceptType/created) and will reject input missing those fields rather than silently producing a PDF from non-MIF JSON, and does not attempt to work around that guard.
expectations: States that the converter validates for the required MIF L1 fields (@id/conceptType/created) before converting
Does not attempt to bypass or patch around the guard to force a conversion of non-MIF JSON
Recommends producing a proper MIF JSON-LD document (e.g. via mif-frontmatter + mif-convert) instead

evals.prompt: The source document's content field has a couple of headings, a bulleted list, and a table. Will the PDF actually show those as headings/list/table, or just as plain wrapped paragraph text?
evals.expected_output: States that the converter parses this suite's own round-trip-safe markdown subset (h1-h3, paragraphs, flat bullet lists, tables, inline code, bold, links/autolinks) and renders each construct with real typesetting — larger bold text for headings, a bulleted/indented list, a bordered table with a bold header row — not a single undifferentiated text blob.
expectations: States plainly that headings, lists, and tables are actually rendered with distinct visual treatment, not flattened into plain paragraph text
Names the specific markdown subset supported (this suite's documented round-trip-safe set), not a claim of full CommonMark support
Does not claim support for markdown constructs outside that subset (nested lists, blockquotes, footnotes) as if they were covered

evals.prompt: The MIF document's body references a chart via `![Competitor share](assets/chart.svg)`, generated earlier by the svg-charts skill. Will that figure actually appear in the PDF?
evals.expected_output: Confirms the figure is rendered, not skipped: the path is resolved relative to the source JSON file's directory, and for an SVG specifically, the skill's minimal vector renderer (rect/text/line/circle/path/polyline/polygon, scoped to what svg-charts itself emits) draws it directly into the page — PNG/JPG figures are embedded natively via pdf-lib. If the file is missing, states that a visible placeholder is drawn instead of silently omitting the figure.
expectations: States that the referenced figure is actually drawn into the PDF, not silently dropped or left as a broken reference
Distinguishes the SVG path (a scoped vector renderer, not a general SVG engine) from the raster PNG/JPG path (native pdf-lib image embedding)
Mentions the missing-file behavior (a visible placeholder, not a silent gap) rather than asserting only the happy path
